content.text: Every production GenAI system generates far more feedback than any engineering team can act on. The feedback-to-backlog process is the mechanism that converts raw signals — thumbs-down ratings, SLO breaches, support escalations, eval regressions — into a ranked, owned, time-bounded engineering queue. Without this process, the loudest voice wins and systemic quality issues accumulate invisibly.

attrs.alt: Feedback-to-backlog prioritization funnel: raw signals pass through triage and RICE scoring into a prioritized backlog with P0-P3 lanes

content.text: Why a Prioritization Process Is Necessary
Feedback volume for a production GenAI system typically exceeds what teams intuitively expect:

A team that triages feedback ad-hoc will consistently over-index on user thumbs-down (high volume, low signal) and under-index on eval regressions (low volume, high signal). A structured process normalizes across sources.
All feedback sources must flow into a single queue with a common schema before triage can begin. This prevents each team from maintaining its own silo.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Literal
from datetime import datetime

FeedbackSource = Literal[
    "user_rating",
    "csat_comment",
    "support_ticket",
    "slo_breach",
    "postmortem_item",
    "eval_regression",
    "sre_capacity_signal",
]

@dataclass
class FeedbackSignal:
    id: str
    source: FeedbackSource
    title: str
    description: str
    affected_component: str      # "inference", "rag", "routing", "prompt", "data"
    raw_severity: str            # P0/P1/P2/P3, set by source system
    estimated_users_affected: int
    created_at: datetime
    metadata: dict = field(default_factory=dict)

def normalize_support_ticket(ticket: dict) -> FeedbackSignal:
    """
    Converts a raw Jira ticket into the common FeedbackSignal schema.
    """
    severity_map = {"blocker": "P0", "critical": "P1", "major": "P2", "minor": "P3"}
    return FeedbackSignal(
        id=f"ticket-{ticket['id']}",
        source="support_ticket",
        title=ticket["summary"],
        description=ticket["description"],
        affected_component=ticket.get("component", "unknown"),
        raw_severity=severity_map.get(ticket["priority"], "P3"),
        estimated_users_affected=ticket.get("affected_customer_count", 1),
        created_at=datetime.fromisoformat(ticket["created"]),
        metadata={"jira_url": ticket["url"], "reporter": ticket["reporter"]},
    )

def normalize_eval_regression(regression: dict) -> FeedbackSignal:
    """
    Converts an eval regression event into the common FeedbackSignal schema.
    """
    delta = regression["before_score"] - regression["after_score"]
    severity = "P1" if delta > 0.05 else "P2" if delta > 0.02 else "P3"
    return FeedbackSignal(
        id=f"eval-{regression['run_id']}-{regression['metric']}",
        source="eval_regression",
        title=f"Eval regression: {regression['metric']} dropped {delta:.1%}",
        description=(
            f"Metric {regression['metric']} dropped from "
            f"{regression['before_score']:.3f} to {regression['after_score']:.3f} "
            f"on dataset {regression['dataset']}."
        ),
        affected_component=regression.get("component", "inference"),
        raw_severity=severity,
        estimated_users_affected=regression.get("affected_query_count", 0),
        created_at=datetime.fromisoformat(regression["detected_at"]),
        metadata={"eval_run_url": regression["run_url"]},
    )

The  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'estimated_users_affected', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}  field is the most important normalization step. A support ticket from an enterprise customer who represents 40,000 seat licenses should score differently from a ticket for an individual free-tier user, even if the symptoms are identical.
Stage 2 — Triage: Dedup, Cluster, and Filter
Multiple feedback signals often point to the same root cause. Filing them as separate backlog items fragments engineering attention and inflates queue size. Deduplication must happen before scoring.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def cluster_signals(signals: list[dict], model: str = "claude-sonnet-5") -> list[dict]:
    """
    Groups semantically related signals into clusters.
    Each cluster will become one backlog item.
    Returns a list of clusters, each with a merged title and the source signal IDs.
    """
    signal_summaries = [
        {"id": s["id"], "title": s["title"], "component": s["affected_component"]}
        for s in signals
    ]
    response = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=2048,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                "Group these feedback signals by shared root cause. "
                "Signals about the same underlying problem should be in the same cluster, "
                "even if the symptom descriptions differ. "
                "Return JSON: a list of objects, each with 'cluster_title' (the root cause "
                "as a 5-10 word engineering task title) and 'signal_ids' (list of IDs). "
                "Every signal must appear in exactly one cluster.

"
                + json.dumps(signal_summaries, indent=2)
            ),
        }],
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

In practice, 100 raw signals per week commonly reduce to 15–25 clusters after deduplication. The reduction ratio is itself a metric: a suddenly lower ratio (say, 3:1 instead of 6:1) signals an unusual diversity of problems requiring investigation.
Not every signal is actionable. The triage step must explicitly filter out signals that should not enter the backlog:
User complaints that the model 'won't do X' when X is by design
Close with explanation, no backlog item
Ticket that matches a P1 item opened two weeks ago
Link to existing item, increment affected count
Out of scope for this team
Complaint about billing, not AI quality
Thumbs-down with no context, no conversation log
Request more info or discard after 7 days
One-off latency spike with no recurrence in 48h
Log but do not create backlog item
The raw severity from the source system is a starting point. The triage step applies override rules to normalize across sources:
Any signal flagged as a safety regression is automatically escalated to P0, regardless of source severity
Any signal affecting more than 5% of weekly active users is escalated to P1
Any third consecutive signal with the same root cause category in a rolling 30-day window is escalated one severity level
Enterprise customer escalations with an active SLA are always treated as P1 or higher

RICE (Reach, Impact, Confidence, Effort) is a scoring framework that produces a comparable numeric score across heterogeneous feedback types. It prevents gut-feel prioritization and makes trade-offs visible.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class RICEScore:
    reach: int              # estimated users affected per week
    impact: float           # 0.25 (minimal) | 0.5 | 1.0 | 2.0 | 3.0 (massive)
    confidence: float       # 0.5 (low) | 0.8 (medium) | 1.0 (high)
    effort: float           # engineering-days to resolve
    safety_multiplier: float = 1.0    # 3.0 for safety issues, 1.0 otherwise
    regulatory_flag: bool = False      # True if non-compliance risk

    @property
    def score(self) -> float:
        base = (self.reach * self.impact * self.confidence) / max(self.effort, 0.5)
        return base * self.safety_multiplier

    def __str__(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"RICE={self.score:.1f} "
            f"(R={self.reach}, I={self.impact}, C={self.confidence}, E={self.effort}d"
            + (" SAFETY" if self.safety_multiplier > 1.0 else "")
            + (" REGULATORY" if self.regulatory_flag else "")
            + ")"
        )

# Example scoring comparison
items = [
    ("Fix p99 latency regression", RICEScore(reach=8000, impact=2.0, confidence=0.8, effort=3.0)),
    ("Improve citation accuracy", RICEScore(reach=2000, impact=1.0, confidence=0.5, effort=8.0)),
    ("Safety: hallucinated medical dosage", RICEScore(reach=150, impact=3.0, confidence=1.0, effort=2.0, safety_multiplier=3.0)),
    ("Improve tone in error messages", RICEScore(reach=5000, impact=0.25, confidence=0.8, effort=1.0)),
]

for title, score in sorted(items, key=lambda x: x[1].score, reverse=True):
    print(f"{score.score:6.0f} | {title}")
    print(f"       {score}")

Running these examples shows that the safety item (reach 150, but 3× safety multiplier) scores 675 — second only to the latency fix at 4,267. Without the multiplier, it would score 225 and fall below the tone improvement item, which would be wrong.
Effort Estimation for GenAI Work Items
Effort estimation for GenAI feedback items follows different rules than standard software tasks. Components with high experimentation cost should be estimated accordingly:
Data prep + train + eval + safety eval
Integration + eval suite + load test + shadow run
Eval runs are part of the effort for any GenAI change. A prompt revision that takes 2 hours to write but requires 3 days of eval review cycles has an effort of 3.5 days, not 0.25 days.
The prioritized backlog is organized into four severity lanes with different response SLAs:
SLO miss, enterprise SLA breach, score > 1000

## Backlog Item: [FB-{id}] {title}

**Source signals:** [list of signal IDs that were merged into this item]
**Severity:** P1
**RICE score:** 2,133 (R=8000, I=2.0, C=0.8, E=3.0d)
**Affected component:** rag / retrieval
**Owner:** @engineer-name
**Sprint:** 2026-Q3-S02
**SLA:** Resolved by 2026-07-14

### Problem statement
[1–3 sentences: what is broken, for whom, how often]

### Root cause hypothesis
[What the triage team believes is causing this]

### Success criteria
[How we will know this is fixed — must include an eval metric or SLO target]

### Proposed fix
[High-level approach, not implementation details]

### Affected signals
- [Link to SLO breach artifact or support ticket]
- [Link to postmortem action item]
- [Link to eval regression run]

The  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'Success criteria', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  field is mandatory. A backlog item without a measurable success criterion cannot be closed — it either gets resolved when someone judges it "good enough" (subjective) or stays open indefinitely.

import httpx

def create_backlog_item(
    cluster: dict,
    rice: "RICEScore",
    severity: str,
    jira_project: str = "GENAI",
    jira_base_url: str = "https://jira.company.com",
    jira_token: str = "",
) -> str:
    """
    Creates a Jira issue for a feedback cluster and returns the issue key.
    """
    priority_map = {"P0": "Blocker", "P1": "Critical", "P2": "Major", "P3": "Minor"}
    description = (
        f"*Source signals:* {', '.join(cluster['signal_ids'])}

"
        f"*RICE score:* {rice.score:.0f} "
        f"(R={rice.reach}, I={rice.impact}, C={rice.confidence}, E={rice.effort}d)

"
        f"*Problem statement:* {cluster['description']}

"
        f"*Root cause hypothesis:* {cluster.get('root_cause_hypothesis', 'TBD')}

"
        f"*Success criteria:* {cluster.get('success_criteria', 'TBD — must be defined before sprint start')}"
    )
    payload = {
        "fields": {
            "project": {"key": jira_project},
            "summary": f"[{severity}] {cluster['cluster_title']}",
            "description": description,
            "issuetype": {"name": "Story"},
            "priority": {"name": priority_map[severity]},
            "labels": ["feedback-driven", cluster["affected_component"]],
        }
    }
    response = httpx.post(
        f"{jira_base_url}/rest/api/2/issue",
        json=payload,
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {jira_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    )
    return response.json()["key"]

The backlog must be reviewed on a fixed weekly cadence, not only when items are created. Without a recurring review, P3 items accumulate indefinitely and P2 items drift past their intended resolution window.

## Weekly Feedback-to-Backlog Review — 30-min agenda

**Attendees:** Product Engineering lead (facilitator), PM, SRE lead, Support lead

### 1. New items this week (10 min)
- Review items created since last meeting
- Confirm RICE scores and severity assignments
- Assign owners for any unowned P1/P2 items

### 2. SLA compliance check (5 min)
- Any P0 items older than 24h unresolved? (should be zero)
- Any P1 items approaching 72h SLA?
- Re-prioritize sprint if needed

### 3. P3 icebox quarterly purge (5 min, monthly)
- Archive items with score < 10 that are > 90 days old
- Re-score items that gained signal since they were triaged

### 4. Success criteria validation (5 min)
- Any items in "in-progress" status without defined success criteria?
- Block from sprint closure until criteria are defined

### 5. Trend analysis (5 min)
- Is any root cause category appearing in 3+ items this week?
- Flag as systemic issue if so → separate program-level item

The trend analysis step is the most valuable part. Three P2 items in the same quarter with  {'type': 'text', 'text': "root_cause_category: 'data'", 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}  should not produce three separate backlog items — it should produce one program-level initiative: a data quality program.
The prioritization process itself must be measured. If items accumulate in triage without reaching the backlog, or if P2 items consistently slip past their SLA, the process is failing.
A structured feedback-to-backlog process prevents the loudest signal from always winning engineering attention. The key components are:
— normalize all feedback sources into a single queue before triage
— merge 100 raw signals into 15–25 clusters to reflect true root cause diversity
RICE scoring with a safety multiplier
— makes safety issues visible even when their raw reach is low
— any GenAI change that requires an eval run must include that cost in the effort estimate
— no item closes without a measurable outcome defined upfront
— a living backlog must be actively maintained, not just appended to
— three signals with the same root cause category trigger a program-level response, not three tickets
